# I Am a Cat — Sample Transcript 01
### *A Night in the Life of Toulouse*

*Generated by the DAVE RPG Engine (Phase 1, claude-sonnet-4-6) on 2026-05-19.
Player: E. Dalton. Toulouse is a large, grey-muzzled domestic cat. It is 3am.*

---

**> where am I**

The living room belongs to the night, and the night belongs to you. Pale light
falls through the windows in long, cool shapes across the floor — not warm, not
interesting in themselves, but they shift when something moves outside, which
means they bear watching. The couch arm sits exactly where it should,
upholstered and flat and positioned with the kind of geographic precision that
suggests the universe occasionally gets things right. The cat tree waits near
the wall, patient as furniture has to be. Across the floor, distributed
according to a logic that is yours and therefore correct, the toys: the soft
mouse, the jingle ball, the chirping thing, the spring that has ambitions toward
the stairs. All present. All potentially relevant. The large dark rectangle on
the wall is doing nothing, which is its whole problem as an object. Somewhere
deeper in the house, the humans are making their slow useless sounds, the ones
that mean sleep — which is their choice to make and also, frankly, their loss.
You sit with your tail arranged just so, and you are curious about several
things at once, which is the correct number of things to be curious about.

---

**> sniff the chirping thing**

The chirping thing smells like nothing that has ever been alive — and yet. You
press your nose closer, whiskers fanning wide, and beneath the aggressive
wrongness of plastic and the faint sharp-edged bite of something electrical,
beneath the accumulated testimony of every paw-bat and floor-skid this object
has ever suffered, there is a thread of something else entirely. Something that
does not quite resolve. Something that sits at the very edge of *bird* or
*beetle* without committing to either, as though whatever made this object was
attempting a forgery and nearly — *nearly* — got away with it. Your whiskers
graze the casing and the toy answers with a single tick, a tiny mechanical
confession, and you hold very still. Interesting. Suspicious. Worth further
investigation.

---

**> tap the chirping thing lightly with my paw**

One measured tap. That is all it takes. Your paw descends with the particular
gravity of a cat who has decided to condescend to interact with something, and
the toy answers immediately — a warbling, wobbling chirp that staggers out of it
like a small bird attempting to communicate through a tunnel, low and wavering
and *wrong* in a way that is, you must admit, substantially more interesting
than a normal chirp would be. It sounds almost desperate. Plaintive. Like prey
that has run very far and is now very tired. You sit with this information for
a moment, tail making one slow, considering arc behind you. *Mrrrp.* You tap
it again.

---

**> pick up the chirping thing**

The toy is in your mouth. This is correct. This is how it should be. You have
performed the maneuver ten thousand times — the precise dip of the broad grey
muzzle, the unhurried opening of the jaw, the authoritative *chomp* — and it has
never once failed you, because you are excellent at this specifically. The toy
emits a wavering, slightly unwell chirp against your teeth, its battery clearly
in some terminal phase of its existence, and the vibration travels pleasantly
along your gumline in a way that a toy in full health, frankly, could not offer.
You stand with it, ears forward, tail making one slow, considered movement. You
have the thing. Now the question, ancient and bottomless, opens before you:
*what is the thing for.*

---

**> go upstairs**

The toy makes a deeply satisfying *mrrp* against your teeth with every step — a
muffled, battery-worn chirp that is almost certainly a cry for help, and that is
exactly as it should be. Up the stairs you go, unhurried, authoritative, a large
cat with cargo and a general sense of direction, your paws finding each riser
with the quiet confidence of a creature who has never once considered that
stairs might be difficult. The upper hallway receives you in dim, carpeted
silence. Soft underfoot — genuinely excellent carpet, the kind that accepts a
crouch — and at the far end, a thin stripe of light leaks under the bedroom door
like a secret being badly kept. The small cat tree stands sentinel near the
wall, its top platform surveying the hallway with the same commanding detachment
you yourself project. You set the toy down for a moment, just to confirm it is
still yours, and it chirps once, feebly, in acknowledgment. The dangling
charging cable near the landing registers in your peripheral awareness as *a
matter for later.* The bedroom door is closed. Everything is exactly as
complicated as it needs to be.

---

**> take the chirping thing into the bedroom**

The toy makes a sound like a small, indignant insect even through your teeth —
muffled, but still *there*, still satisfying in a low-grade, jaw-deep sort of
way. You carry it the full length of the hallway with the measured dignity of a
cat delivering something important, which you are, and you arrive at the bedroom
door to find it exactly as doors always are at this hour: closed, inconsiderate,
and faintly lit along the bottom edge like it's trying to apologize. You bump
your nose against the wood anyway, just to confirm. The confirmation is: yes,
still a door. So you sit. You arrange yourself squarely in front of it, toy
held a moment longer before you set it down with deliberate precision on the
soft worn carpet, and you stare at the thin line of light beneath the door with
the absolute, unhurried confidence of a cat who has done this before and knows,
with deep evolutionary certainty, how it ends. The slow breathing inside
continues, oblivious. The fan hums its useless white-noise hum. Somewhere on the
other side of that door, two humans are making the unfortunate choice of
remaining asleep, unaware that a gift has arrived. You are *expectant*.

---

**> WAKE UP YOU SILLY HUMANS!**

The yowl builds perfectly in your chest — you can feel it, resonant and
authoritative, the kind of sound that cuts through doors and dreams and
unreasonable human indifference to the concept of breakfast. Your mouth opens.
And then your stomach has a completely different opinion. What follows is not a
yowl. What follows is a series of undignified lurching contractions that
commandeer your entire body from the inside, and then — with a sound somewhere
between a wet cough and a small catastrophe — the hairball arrives on the carpet
with a finality that requires no translation in any language, feline or
otherwise. You sit very still for a moment, blinking. The pressure that has been
quietly colonising your ribcage for the past several hours is simply *gone*,
which is honestly a relief, even if the circumstances are beneath your dignity.
From behind the bedroom door: a sharp exhale, the long creak of a mattress
taking someone's weight, and then footsteps — deliberate, not hurried, the
footsteps of a human who is awake but has not yet decided to be reasonable about
it. The door swings open. One human fills the frame, squinting against the dark,
holding their phone aloft so the light falls directly onto you, and then — with
the slow inevitability of someone who already suspects what they're going to
find — onto the carpet beside you. The sound they make is low and textured and
carries a very clear emotional weight even if the syllables mean nothing. You
arrange yourself into a posture of dignified attentiveness, tail curled over
your paws, and look up at them. *You are awake,* you observe, with a small,
composed *mrp.* This was the goal. The method is perhaps not worth discussing.

---

**> take chirping thing into bedroom**

The chirping thing gives a muffled, slightly throttled squeak between your teeth
as you clear the doorway — a deeply satisfying sound, like a small surrender —
and you carry it into the bedroom with your tail held high and perpendicular,
the universal flag of a cat who has somewhere important to be. The room is dim
and warm and smells of sleep: the specific, dense, slightly stale warmth of
humans who have been horizontal for hours and have no idea what is about to
happen to them. The carpet is soft under your paws. A machine somewhere hums its
meaningless white noise hum. The bed looms large and rumpled, a terrain of
blankets and limbs, and you pause just inside the door for one precise moment —
toy clamped, tail up, pupils wide and luminous in the dark — to fully appreciate
the strategic landscape before you. You have a mission. You have a toy. The toy
makes noise. These facts are in perfect alignment.

---

**> jump up on bed**

The leap is effortless — haunches coiling, then releasing, and then you are
simply *there*, four paws planted in the deep softness of the mattress with a
satisfying, authoritative thud. The chirping thing makes a small, muffled sound
against your teeth as you land, a secondary announcement to no one in
particular. Warmth radiates from both sides of you in large, slow-breathing
shapes, and the one on the left shifts with a murmur — a low, rumbling sound
that means nothing, curves upward at the end, and then dissolves back into the
humid stillness of human sleep. You stand in the middle of it all, considerable,
sphinx-like, the chirping thing clamped between your jaws, and the bed stretches
away from you in every direction like a territory freshly annexed.

---

**> drop the chirping thing on the mama**

The chirping thing lands on the mama with a deeply satisfying *thump*, and then
— one final, outraged *chirp* against the blanket, as though it has something to
say about all of this. You sit back, tail curled neatly around your paws, and
wait. She *moves*. One eye opens — large, soft, still blurred at the edges with
sleep — and finds you there, approximately four inches from her face, radiating
the composed dignity of someone who has done an important thing and requires it
to be acknowledged. You meet her eye with the full weight of your attention. A
short, precise *mrrp* — not a question, not a demand, simply a notification:
*the package has been delivered; please process.* Her mouth does something warm
and shapeless, a low rumble of sound that washes over you in a wave of amused
softness, and somewhere inside it, nothing useful penetrates — not a single word
you need — but the *tone* of it lands just fine. She knows. She sees you. The
one open eye is looking at you the way eyes look at something they are not
entirely displeased to see, even now, even at whatever hour this is that
apparently does not have a name you care about. You wait. Expectant. The
chirping thing sits silently against her arm, ready to be useful again at a
moment's notice. You are also ready.

---

**> bump the mama's face with head**

Your forehead connects with her cheek and you hold it there — a pronouncement,
really, a formal declaration in the ancient language of foreheads — and the
warmth of her skin bleeds through your fur in a way that is, fine, objectively
pleasant, even if pleasure is not the *point* right now. The point is
*breakfast*. She makes a sound, low and rumbly, half-groan and half-something-
else, and then — *then* — her hand arrives on your back like a slow, large,
deeply satisfying cloud, dragging from your neck all the way to the base of your
tail in one long stroke that you did not ask for and do not, strictly speaking,
require. You allow it. She has not gotten up. The bowl remains empty somewhere
in the dark beyond this room, a fact you hold in your chest like a small, urgent
coal. But her eyes are open now, catching the faint light, and you can feel her
sleepiness loosening its grip — a shift in her breathing, a new quality of
stillness, the particular way a human lies still when they are *awake* and
merely *pretending* to be otherwise. You know this. You are a *professional*. A
small sound escapes you, soft and ascending, the shape of *soon, though, right?*

---

**> on the other hand, maybe a nap would be good. Purrr.**

The kneading happens before you quite decide to do it — two slow presses into
the mattress, your paws working the give of it on pure instinct, and then your
body simply proceeds with the plan your body has apparently made: one unhurried
rotation, nose briefly grazing the warm valley between the two large sleeping
shapes, and then down, folded, loaf. The purr arrives the same way, uninvited
and unannounced, a low machinery starting up somewhere behind your sternum
without consulting you at all. You did not authorize this. And yet here you
are — tucked into the warmth, the purr doing its thing, and the mama's hand
finding your flank in the dark with the unerring accuracy of long habit,
settling there without her so much as shifting her breathing. It is, you note
distantly, quite warm. The chirping toy is somewhere near the foot of the bed.
It can wait.

---

*End of session.*
